meta.name: French Country Braised Carrots
meta.subtitle: Tender carrots glazed with butter, a touch of honey, and fresh herbs.

source.name: ssa.cuisines.french_country
notes: Canonical SSA side dish: French-style braised/glazed carrots (carottes glacées).
Designed for deterministic timing, easy scaling, and batch/holiday service holding.

summary: French country braised carrots: carrots gently simmered with butter and a splash of stock, then reduced into a glossy glaze with herbs. Sweetness is optional and kept subtle.

yield.notes: Serves 6 as a side. Double recipe using a wider pan for even glazing.

notes: Best served fresh; holds warm well for 20–30 minutes.
Reheats nicely with a splash of water/stock.
Butter-glazed, herb-fresh, lightly sweet (optional).
Classic French finish: glossy glaze, not saucy.

optional.label: Cast iron skillet
optional.notes: Great heat retention; watch reduction to prevent scorching.

optional.label: Small saucepan
optional.notes: Useful if you prefer to warm stock or melt butter separately.

notes: Contains butter. Use olive oil or plant butter for dairy-free.

ingredients.label: Carrots, peeled (or scrubbed) and cut into batons or thick coins
notes: Cut evenly for consistent braise time.
Batons glaze beautifully; coins are quicker.

ingredients.label: Chicken stock (or vegetable stock)
notes: Water works, but stock adds depth.

ingredients.label: Kosher salt
notes: Start lighter; reduce glaze concentrates salt.

ingredients.label: Honey (optional, for a gentle glaze)
notes: Traditional glazed carrots often use a small pinch of sugar; honey is SSA-friendly.

ingredients.label: Fresh thyme (or dried thyme)
notes: If using fresh, 3–4 sprigs is typical. If dried, use 1/2 tsp.

ingredients.label: Lemon zest (tiny pinch, optional brighten)
notes: Add at the end; a little goes a long way.

ingredients.label: Lemon juice (optional, 1–2 tsp to balance sweetness)

steps.title: Prep carrots for even cooking
instructions: Peel or scrub carrots. Trim ends.
Cut into even batons (about finger-thick) or thick coins (~1/2-inch).
Keep pieces similar size so they finish together.
checks: No very thin pieces that will overcook during braise.

steps.title: Start the braise with butter and stock
instructions: Add carrots to a wide skillet.
Add butter, stock, salt, thyme, and optional bay leaf.
Bring to a simmer over medium heat.
checks: Liquid is simmering gently (not vigorously boiling).

steps.title: Cover and braise until just tender
instructions: Cover with a lid (or foil) and simmer gently 10–14 minutes.
Stir once or twice for even cooking.
Carrots should be tender but still hold their shape.
checks: Knife slides in with light resistance; carrots are not mushy.

steps.title: Uncover and reduce into a glossy glaze
instructions: Remove lid. Increase heat to medium-high to reduce the liquid.
If using honey, add now and toss to coat.
Cook, tossing occasionally, until the liquid thickens and coats carrots in a shiny glaze.
checks: Pan liquid is syrupy and coats a spoon lightly.
Carrots look glossy, not swimming in liquid.

steps.title: Finish with herbs and balance
instructions: Remove bay leaf and thyme sprigs if used whole.
Taste and adjust salt and pepper.
Optional: add a pinch of lemon zest and/or 1–2 tsp lemon juice to brighten.
Finish with chopped parsley if desired. Serve warm.
checks: Flavor is balanced: buttery, lightly sweet (if used), and fresh.
Glaze remains glossy (not broken or greasy).

notes: A classic side for roast chicken or beef stew.
Also pairs well with simple lentils and a green salad.
Cool quickly; store airtight.
Glaze will set slightly when cold—normal.
Skillet: reheat over medium-low with 1–2 tbsp water/stock; toss until glossy again.
Microwave: cover and heat in short bursts; finish with a small pat of butter if needed.
Hold warm (covered) up to 30 minutes; re-gloss with a splash of stock and quick toss if needed.
A wide pan helps reduction glaze evenly—avoid crowded carrots.
If glaze looks greasy, add 1–2 tsp water and toss over heat to re-emulsify.
For deeper flavor, swap half the stock for a dry white wine (if your household uses it).
Replace 2 tbsp stock with orange juice; finish with orange zest.
Keep sweetness subtle; reduce carefully to avoid burning sugars.
Use olive oil or plant butter in place of butter.
Finish with a drizzle of good olive oil and extra herbs.
Skip honey; rely on carrot natural sweetness.
Add a tiny splash of lemon juice at the end for balance.

remixIdeas: Chop and fold into rice or couscous-style bowls
Add to beef stew near the end for a sweet veg accent
Serve cold as a salad with vinaigrette and herbs
